563	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
564	
565	> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the one invariant — exactly one ordered writer per symbol, money strongly consistent, everything else eventual — and reason forward from there.
566	
567	### How do we keep per-symbol matching correct AND fast under a hot symbol?
568	
569	#### Bad: One big locked order book
570	**🗣️ Plain words:** put every symbol's book in one big table and lock a row whenever you match.
571	**Approach:** a single shared order-book table; each match takes a row lock, reads resting orders, writes the fill.
572	**Why people try this:** it's the obvious "just use the database" answer.
573	**⚠️ What breaks:** at ~27K orders/s with locks held across read-match-write, the connection pool (the fixed set of DB connections, ~100-200) exhausts in seconds; worse, price-time priority isn't even guaranteed because lock acquisition order ≠ arrival order. Correctness AND throughput both fail.
574	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the very first burst — and the fairness bug is fatal regardless of load.
575	
576	#### Good: In-memory single-writer per symbol
577	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** the DB-lock approach was both slow and didn't guarantee fair ordering; this fixes ordering by making it a single in-memory thread per book.
578	**🗣️ Plain words:** give each stock its own in-memory queue handler that processes orders one at a time in the exact order they arrived.
579	**Approach:** a sequencer (a component that stamps a strictly increasing number) assigns a global order; each symbol's book lives in memory on one shard, one thread applies events in sequence order. No locks — there's nothing to contend. Every event is appended to a durable log before the fill is acknowledged.
580	**Why this is better:** single-writer makes price-time priority deterministic and removes locking entirely from the hot path; ~50K orders/s/core easily covers the ~8K hottest symbol.
581	**⚠️ What breaks:** if ONE symbol's flow ever approaches ~50K/s (a circuit-breaker reopen, a viral expiry), that single core saturates and orders for that symbol back up — and a single shard crash loses the in-memory book until replay completes.
582	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** a symbol exceeding one core's throughput, plus the need for fast crash recovery.
583	
584	```mermaid
585	flowchart TD
586	    SEQ[Sequencer global order]
587	    SEQ -->|"sym A events"| MEA[Shard A single writer]
588	    SEQ -->|"sym B events"| MEB[Shard B single writer]
589	    MEA -->|"append"| LOG[[Event log]]
590	    MEB -->|"append"| LOG
591	    LOG -.->|"snapshot"| SNAP[(Book snapshots)]
592	```
593	
594	#### Great: Sharded books + price-band splitting + replay recovery
595	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** one core per symbol caps a hot symbol and a crash loses the live book; this adds a way to split a single book and to recover fast.
596	**🗣️ Plain words:** keep one writer per book normally, but if a single contract gets insane, split its book into price bands each with its own writer, and recover a crashed book by replaying its log from the last snapshot.
597	**Approach:** (1) Books are assigned to shards by consistent hashing on symbol so we can rebalance without renumbering everything. (2) A hot symbol is detected by its per-shard QPS; we split its book into non-overlapping **price bands** (e.g. ≥2940 and <2940), each owned by one writer, with a thin coordinator that crosses only at the band boundary — preserving price-time within each band. (3) Recovery: a standby shard replays the event log from the last snapshot to rebuild the book in seconds, then resumes from the sequencer offset.
598	**Why this works:** keeps the single-writer invariant within a band while removing the single-core ceiling for the one symbol that needs it; replay bounds RTO.
599	
600	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** hottest symbol ~8K/s vs single-core ~50K/s = 6.3x headroom. Split triggers if sustained per-symbol QPS > ~40K/s (80% of ceiling). Two price bands roughly double the ceiling to ~100K/s for that one symbol. Replay: at ~50K events/s replay and a 1-min snapshot interval, worst-case ~3M events ≈ ~60s rebuild — within RTO.
601	
602	**✅ Failure matrix:**
603	
604	| Scenario | What happens |
605	|---|---|
606	| Order accepted, shard crashes before match | Replay from log rebuilds book; order is in the log, re-applied |
607	| Match done, log append fails | Fill NOT acknowledged; fail closed, no money moves |
608	| Shard crashes mid-snapshot | Snapshot is atomic-rename; partial snapshot ignored, replay from prior |
609	| Hot symbol > ceiling | Price-band split; if still over, throttle new orders (429) before dropping |
610	| Duplicate sequenced event | Single-writer enforces monotonic seq; duplicate ignored |
611	
612	> 🎙️ Script: "The heart is a single writer per symbol — one in-memory thread applying sequenced orders, no locks, so price-time priority is deterministic and a hot symbol at 8 thousand a second sits comfortably under a 50-thousand-a-second core. For crash safety, every event hits a durable log first and I snapshot books, so a standby rebuilds in under a minute by replay. The one escape hatch is if a single contract goes truly wild — I split its book into price bands, each with its own writer, which keeps ordering correct within a band while doubling the ceiling."
613	
614	> 🧠 If they ask "doesn't price-band splitting break price-time priority?": "Within a band it's fully preserved; orders only cross bands at the boundary, where a thin coordinator serializes the crossing. It's correct as long as bands don't overlap."
615	
616	### How do we move money exactly once and never oversell?
617	
618	#### Bad: Debit cash, then add shares (two writes)
619	**🗣️ Plain words:** first take the money, then in a second step add the shares.
620	**Approach:** two separate writes after a fill.
621	**Why people try this:** simpler than one big transaction.
622	**⚠️ What breaks:** a crash between the two leaves cash gone but no shares — the single worst broker bug. And a redelivered fill double-charges.
623	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the first crash or first redelivery.
624	
625	#### Good: One ACID transaction + dedup
626	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** two writes weren't atomic; wrap them in one transaction and guard against redelivery.
627	**🗣️ Plain words:** move cash and shares together in one all-or-nothing step, and remember which fills you've already processed so a repeat does nothing.
628	**Approach:** in ONE Postgres transaction: debit buyer blocked cash, credit seller, adjust both holdings, and insert the fill_id into a dedup table (unique constraint). At-least-once redelivery hits the unique constraint and no-ops.
629	**⚠️ What breaks:** works when buyer, seller, and symbol live on the same shard — but with user_id sharding, buyer and seller (and their cash/holdings) are often on different shards, so a single transaction can't span them.
630	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** cross-shard fills.
631	
632	```mermaid
633	flowchart TD
634	    LOG[[Event log]]
635	    LOG -->|"fill"| LED[Ledger consumer]
636	    LED -->|"dedup insert"| DD[(Dedup table)]
637	    LED -->|"one txn"| PG[(Funds and holdings)]
638	```
639	
640	#### Great: Cross-shard saga with reservation + compensation
641	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** one transaction can't span shards; use a saga with a pre-reserved guarantee so it can't fail unsafely.
642	**🗣️ Plain words:** because the money was already RESERVED when the order was placed, applying a fill is just confirming reservations on each shard; if one side fails, we compensate (reverse) the other.
643	**Approach:** the funds were blocked at order time (§6.1), so the fill is a series of local "confirm reservation" transactions, one per shard, each idempotent on fill_id. A saga coordinator drives: confirm buyer (cash blocked→spent, shares+), confirm seller (shares−, cash+). If a step fails, run its compensation. Because everything keys on fill_id and funds were pre-reserved, there's no path where a user is charged without the offsetting share movement; worst case is a stuck saga that the reconciliation batch + on-call runbook resolve.
644	
645	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** ~60M fills/month, dedup table at ~50 bytes/row pruned to settlement window — trivial. Pre-reservation means 0% of fills can fail for "insufficient funds" at fill time (already checked at placement).
646	
647	**✅ Failure matrix:**
648	
649	| Scenario | What happens |
650	|---|---|
651	| Buyer shard confirms, seller shard down | Saga retries seller; buyer side is idempotent, no double-apply |
652	| Fill redelivered | Dedup on fill_id no-ops every step |
653	| Crash mid-saga | Saga log replays from last completed step |
654	| Reservation expired/cancelled before fill | Impossible — sequencer ordered cancel vs fill; loser sees 409 |
655	| Reconciliation finds mismatch vs clearing corp | Alert + runbook; ledger is source of truth, manual adjust entry |
656	
657	> 🎙️ Script: "Money moves on a fill in one atomic Postgres transaction with a dedup table, so a redelivered fill never double-charges. Because I shard by user and buyer and seller can be on different shards, I escalate to a saga — but the trick is funds were already reserved when the order was placed, so the fill is just confirming reservations, and there's no way to be charged without the matching share move. A reconciliation batch catches any stuck saga against the clearing corp."
658	
659	> 🧠 If they ask "why not two-phase commit across shards?": "2PC blocks all participants while the coordinator decides — a coordinator stall freezes money writes. A saga with pre-reservation gives the same safety without the blocking, because the risky check already happened at order time."
660	
661	### How do we fan out 60M messages/s without melting under back-pressure?
662	
663	#### Good: Per-symbol topics + coalescing
664	**🗣️ Plain words:** group updates by stock and only send the latest value a few times a second.
665	**Approach:** per-symbol pub/sub topics; coalesce to ~4 frames/s (last-value-wins). Clients subscribe to only what they watch.
666	**⚠️ What breaks:** a slow client or a saturated gateway can back up; if buffers are unbounded, memory blows and the gateway crashes, taking 50K connections with it.
667	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** a slow-consumer / overload event.
668	
669	```mermaid
670	flowchart TD
671	    PUB[Coalescing pub-sub]
672	    PUB -->|"frames"| WS[WebSocket gateway]
673	    WS -->|"push"| C1[Fast client]
674	    WS -->|"push"| C2[Slow client]
675	```
676	
677	#### Great: Bounded buffers + load-shedding order
678	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** unbounded buffering is a latent outage; bound it and shed in a defined order.
679	**🗣️ Plain words:** give each client a small fixed mailbox; if it overflows, drop stale quotes for that client (never the money path), and if the whole gateway is hot, shed market-data before anything else.
680	**Approach:** each connection has a bounded ring buffer; on overflow, drop the oldest (last-value-wins makes this safe — the client just skips to the current price). Load-shedding order under overload: (1) drop coalesced market-data frames for slow clients, (2) widen coalescing cadence globally (4→1 frame/s), (3) reject NEW subscriptions with 429 + Retry-After — but NEVER touch order placement or money. Trigger signal: gateway queue depth / consumer lag / p99 push latency.
681	
682	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 60M msgs/s ÷ 50K conns/node = ~20 nodes; each node ~1.2M msgs/s. A bounded buffer of ~64 frames/client at 60B = ~4KB/client, ~200MB/node — bounded and safe.
683	
684	**✅ Failure matrix:**
685	
686	| Scenario | What happens |
687	|---|---|
688	| One slow client | Its ring buffer drops oldest; it skips to current price |
689	| Gateway saturates | Widen coalescing globally, then 429 new subs; orders unaffected |
690	| Gateway crashes | Clients reconnect elsewhere, get cache snapshot, resume |
691	| Exchange feed drops | Serve last-value from cache, flag stale on UI |
692	
693	> 🎙️ Script: "Fan-out is decoupled per-symbol pub/sub with coalescing to a few frames a second. The senior move is bounding every client's buffer — last-value-wins means dropping a stale quote is harmless — and defining a strict shed order: drop market-data first, widen the cadence, reject new subscriptions with a 429, and never, ever touch the order or money path. Twenty gateways at fifty thousand connections each carry the whole firehose."
694	
695	> 🧠 If they ask "what about the idempotency / sequencer being a single coordinator?": "The sequencer is the one true global coordinator — it's a hot in-memory stamper backed by the log, partitioned so a standby can take over from the last logged sequence number; if it's down we fail closed and reject orders rather than accept anything unordered."
696	
697	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
698	
699	### 9A — Availability targets
700	- **Money/write path: 99.99% during market hours.** Four nines is ~52 min/year total, but concentrated into the ~6 daily market hours, so even seconds at the open matter. Mechanisms: multi-AZ Postgres (synchronous replica in a second data center in the same region so a building failure auto-fails-over), the durable event log replicated 3x, single-writer shards with hot standbys ready to replay.
701	- **Market-data read path: 99.9%** (~8.7 hrs/year). Mechanisms: stateless WebSocket fleet behind a load balancer, last-value cache so a gateway loss is a reconnect, not data loss.
702	- *Multi-AZ* = multiple data centers in one region; if one loses power the other takes over automatically.
703	
704	### 9B — Per-dependency failure table
705	
706	| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER sees) | Recovery |
707	|---|---|---|---|
708	| Matching shard | A symbol stops matching | That symbol's orders queue; user sees "accepted, pending" — others trade fine | Standby replays log from snapshot (~60s) |
709	| Sequencer | No new orders can be ordered | New orders rejected with a clear "try again" — open positions still readable | Standby resumes from last logged seq |
710	| Funds/holdings PG (primary) | No money moves | Order placement rejected (fail closed); portfolio reads still served from replica/cache | Sync replica promoted (multi-AZ) |
711	| Redis (portfolio cache) | Slower portfolio reads | Reads fall through to replica — slightly slower, still correct | Re-warm from PG |
712	| Market-data cache | No reconnect snapshots | Reconnecting clients wait for next tick (sub-second) | Re-warm from pub/sub |
713	| WebSocket gateway | 50K clients drop | Clients auto-reconnect to another gateway, get snapshot | Stateless; LB reroutes |
714	| Exchange feed | No fresh prices | Prices freeze, UI flags "delayed"; matching of OUR internal book still works | Failover to backup feed line |
715	| Event log | Catastrophic | System fails closed — refuse new orders rather than risk losing one | Restore from 3x replicas |
716	
717	### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class
718	*RPO* = how much data we'd lose if it crashed right now (RPO≈0 = lose nothing, every write synchronously durable before ack). *RTO* = how long until the service works again.
719	
720	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
721	|---|---|---|---|
722	| Orders / fills / funds | ≈0 | < ~1 min | Sync multi-AZ replica + 3x event log; ack only after durable |
723	| Order books (in-memory) | ≈0 (in log) | < ~60s | Replay from log + last snapshot |
724	| Portfolio cache | minutes OK (rebuildable) | seconds | Re-warm from PG |
725	| Market-data / ticks | seconds OK | seconds | Rebuildable from exchange feed |
726	
727	### 9D — Cost
728	- **#1 driver — market-data egress (~$143K/mo):** every quote frame pushed to every client is billed per byte; at ~60M msgs/s this dwarfs everything. **Biggest optimization:** the coalescing already cuts it ~12x; a compact binary frame (~60B) over a verbose JSON (~700B) is another ~10x — egress is where every cost dollar should go.
729	- **#2 — compute (~$60K/mo):** ~200 VMs (matching shards, ~20 WebSocket gateways, API tier, PG primaries+replicas, Redis). Optimization: matching shards idle overnight — autoscale the read edge to near-zero off-hours.
730	- **#3 — storage (~$1K/mo):** trivial; orders ~54TB/yr and ticks ~34TB/yr compressed in cheap cold storage.
731	- **Total ~$200K/mo; ~$154 per 1M order events.**
732	
733	**Observability:** golden signals — latency (order-accept p99, push p99), traffic (order QPS, fan-out msgs/s), errors (reject rate, saga failures), saturation (per-shard QPS vs ceiling, gateway buffer depth). Burn-rate alerts on the 99.99% money SLO. Trace-id propagated gateway → sequencer → matching shard → event log → ledger consumer, so one order is traceable across the async hop.
734	
735	**Back-pressure:** hottest async path is the fan-out — bounded per-client ring buffers, shed market-data first, never the money path (§8). **Rate-limiting:** order endpoint is token-bucket per user; on breach 429 + Retry-After. The limiter state is itself a global coordinator (Redis), so it's sized and replicated like one.
736	
737	> 🎙️ Script: "When a matching shard dies, that one symbol pauses — you see 'accepted, pending' while a standby replays in under a minute, and every other symbol keeps trading. If the funds DB primary fails we fail closed on new orders but you can still read your portfolio from the replica. The one thing we never compromise is the event log — if it's truly gone we refuse new orders rather than risk losing a single trade. And the cost story is blunt: it's almost all market-data egress, so that's where the binary wire format and coalescing earn their keep."
738	
739	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
740	
741	**🔀 Decision: Single-writer per-symbol matching vs lock-based shared book**
742	1. **Chose & why:** single in-memory writer per symbol — deterministic price-time priority with no locking.
743	2. **Gave up:** a single symbol is capped at one core (~50K/s) until we price-band split.
744	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like one cashier per checkout lane — perfectly orderly, but one lane can only go so fast.
745	4. **When this reverses:** if a single symbol routinely needs >100K/s even after splitting (HFT venue scale), we'd move to a specialized hardware/FPGA matcher.
746	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I keep one ordered writer per book because fairness demands it; if one contract ever outgrows a core I split it by price band before reaching for exotic hardware."
747	
748	**🔀 Decision: CP money path (fail closed) vs AP (accept optimistically)**
749	1. **Chose & why:** reject rather than risk a wrong fill or double-charge.
750	2. **Gave up:** a few ms of latency and some availability — an outage means no new orders.
751	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** a slower but guaranteed road over a fast one that might charge you twice.
752	4. **When this reverses:** an HFT/market-maker context where speed is the product — then accept optimistically and reconcile.
753	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For retail money I'd always rather say 'try again' than risk being wrong; if this were HFT I'd flip to optimistic and reconcile."
754	
755	**🔀 Decision: Coalesced market-data (~4/s) vs every raw tick**
756	1. **Chose & why:** ~12x less egress with no user-visible loss.
757	2. **Gave up:** tick-exact streams for the rare client who wants them.
758	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** showing the latest price a few times a second instead of every flicker no one can read.
759	4. **When this reverses:** if we sell a pro/algo feed, offer an uncoalesced tier at a price that covers its egress.
760	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Retail can't read 50 updates a second, so I coalesce to a few — that one choice halves my biggest cost line; pros who need raw ticks pay for a separate feed."
761	
762	**🔀 Decision: Saga + pre-reservation vs 2PC across shards**
763	1. **Chose & why:** non-blocking exactly-once money without a coordinator that can freeze writes.
764	2. **Gave up:** a tiny window where a stuck saga needs reconciliation.
765	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** handing off a baton with an undo step, instead of making everyone wait for one referee.
766	4. **When this reverses:** if we never shard funds (small scale), one Postgres transaction is simpler — drop the saga.
767	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Across shards I use a saga with pre-reserved funds, not two-phase commit, because 2PC blocks money writes if the coordinator stalls."
768	
769	**CAP/PACELC:** the money write path is **CP** — under a partition we sacrifice availability (reject orders) to stay consistent; and PACELC, Else we favor **Latency** for the market-data read edge (eventual, fast) but **Consistency** for the money core.
770	
771	> 🎙️ Script: "My least-certain calls: single-writer matching trades a per-symbol ceiling for fairness, and I'd only reach for exotic hardware past 100K on one symbol. The money path is deliberately CP — I fail closed rather than risk a wrong fill. And coalescing market data is a cost bet that retail doesn't need every tick. If any of those scale assumptions break — HFT speed, a truly viral symbol, or a pro feed product — each one has a named flip."
772	
773	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
774	
775	**❓ Two orders race for the last resting share — who wins?**
776	The mechanism: both are stamped by the sequencer with monotonic numbers; the single-writer for that symbol applies them strictly in sequence order, so whoever's order was sequenced first matches and the other rests or rejects. There's no tie because there's a single total order.
777	🗣️ In plain words: there's one line and one cashier per stock, so being first in line is unambiguous.
778	💬 One-liner: "The sequencer gives one global order, and one writer per book honors it — no race, by construction."
779	
780	**❓ Can the same order be placed twice if the client retries?**
781	The mechanism: every order carries a client idempotency key; the gateway checks a Redis dedup store first and returns the original orderId on a repeat, so a retry never creates a second order. The dedup key has a TTL covering the trading day.
782	🗣️ In plain words: we remember the request id, so hitting "buy" twice on a flaky network still buys once.
783	💬 One-liner: "Idempotency key plus a dedup store — retries return the original order, never a duplicate."
784	
785	**❓ What if the payment/funds DB primary goes down mid-market?**
786	The mechanism: it's multi-AZ with a synchronous replica; we promote the replica (RTO < ~1 min, RPO≈0). While failing over we fail closed on new orders but keep serving portfolio reads from the replica/cache.
787	🗣️ In plain words: there's a hot backup copy of the money DB; if the main one dies we switch to it and lose nothing, just briefly can't place new orders.
788	💬 One-liner: "Sync multi-AZ replica, RPO zero — promote and resume; we reject new orders rather than risk a wrong write."
789	
790	**❓ What if a matching shard crashes with orders in flight?**
791	The mechanism: the in-memory book is reconstructable because every event hit the durable log before acknowledgment; a standby replays from the last snapshot (~60s) and resumes from the sequencer offset. In-flight orders are in the log, so nothing is lost.
792	🗣️ In plain words: the book lives in memory but its history is on disk, so we rebuild it exactly.
793	💬 One-liner: "Snapshot plus log replay rebuilds the book in under a minute — the log is the truth, memory is just fast."
794	
795	**❓ How do you handle a flash event — one option contract going viral on expiry?**
796	The mechanism: that's the hottest-symbol case at ~8K/s today; if it nears the ~50K/s single-core ceiling we price-band split its book across writers, and we rate-limit (429) new orders on it before ever dropping anything.
797	🗣️ In plain words: if one stock gets crazy busy we split its order queue into price ranges, each with its own handler.
798	💬 One-liner: "Single hot symbol has 6x headroom; past that we split the book by price band, not drop orders."
799	
800	**❓ Can you oversell — fill more shares than exist on the book?**
801	The mechanism: matching is single-writer against an in-memory book, so resting quantity is decremented atomically as it matches; there's no concurrent reader to oversell, and the fill is logged before acknowledgment.
802	🗣️ In plain words: one handler per stock counts down the available shares one order at a time, so it can't go negative.
803	💬 One-liner: "One writer per book means quantity decrements are serial — overselling is impossible by design."
804	
805	**❓ Can a user be charged without receiving shares?**
806	The mechanism: a fill applies cash and shares in one ACID transaction (or a saga with pre-reserved funds across shards), guarded by a fill-id dedup table for exactly-once. There's no commit order where cash leaves without shares arriving.
807	🗣️ In plain words: money out and shares in happen together or not at all.
808	💬 One-liner: "Cash and shares move in one atomic step with a dedup guard — never half."
809	
810	**❓ How do you stop a user editing the price in the request to buy cheap?**
811	The mechanism: the client never sends a tradable price for execution against the book — limit price is validated, but the EXECUTION price comes from matching against real resting orders; funds, identity, and balances are all server-side from the JWT and ledger, never trusted from the request.
812	🗣️ In plain words: you can say what you're willing to pay, but the actual price comes from a real seller, not from your request.
813	💬 One-liner: "Execution price comes from the book, identity from the token, funds from our ledger — nothing tradable is trusted from the client."
814	
815	**❓ What if someone forges another user's id to read their portfolio?**
816	The mechanism: identity is taken only from the signed JWT, never a client field; object-level authz checks the caller owns the requested resource before any read or mutation.
817	🗣️ In plain words: we trust the login token, not anything typed in the request, and we check you own what you're asking about.
818	💬 One-liner: "Identity from the JWT, object-level authz on every call — you can only ever touch your own account."
819	
820	**❓ What's the most expensive part of this system?**
821	The mechanism: market-data egress at ~$143K/mo — pushing ~60M quote frames/s to a million clients, billed per byte — dwarfs compute (~$60K) and storage (~$1K). Coalescing and a compact binary wire format are the big levers.
822	🗣️ In plain words: the biggest bill is the bandwidth of streaming live prices to everyone, not the databases.
823	💬 One-liner: "Egress on the price feed is ~70% of cost — coalescing and a binary frame are where I spend the optimization."
824	
825	**❓ How would you cut cost the most?**
826	The mechanism: widen coalescing off-peak, use a compact binary frame (~10x vs JSON), autoscale the read edge to near-zero overnight (markets are closed ~18 hrs/day), and tier ticks to cold storage.
827	🗣️ In plain words: send smaller messages, fewer of them when it's quiet, and turn the read fleet off after market hours.
828	💬 One-liner: "Binary frames plus off-hours autoscale — the market's closed most of the day, so don't pay for an idle edge."
829	
830	**❓ Why eventual consistency for the portfolio but strong for the ledger?**
831	The mechanism: the ledger is money — strong, exactly-once. The portfolio is a display read served from cache, but read-your-own-writes is preserved by invalidating the user's cache on every fill, so YOU always see your own trade even if others' views are ms-stale.
832	🗣️ In plain words: the official money records are exact; the screen can be a heartbeat behind for others but always shows your own trades instantly.
833	💬 One-liner: "Strong where it's money, eventual where it's display — but a fill pops your cache so you read your own writes."
834	
835	**❓ How would you add a new asset class, like mutual funds, later?**
836	The mechanism: mutual funds don't match on an order book (NAV-priced, end-of-day), so they bypass the matching engine entirely and plug into the same funds ledger and holdings model as a different order type with a batch fill at NAV. The ledger and portfolio paths are reused as-is.
837	🗣️ In plain words: funds are priced once a day, not matched live, so they skip the order book but reuse the same money and holdings plumbing.
838	💬 One-liner: "New non-matched asset classes reuse the ledger and holdings; only the 'how it fills' step differs."
839	
840	**❓ How would you support algo/API traders who want raw ticks?**
841	The mechanism: offer a separate uncoalesced market-data tier on its own gateway pool, priced to cover its egress, with per-key rate limits — keeping the retail feed coalesced and cheap.
842	🗣️ In plain words: pros who want every tick get a separate, paid firehose; retail keeps the cheaper trimmed feed.
843	💬 One-liner: "A separate priced raw-tick tier — pros pay for the egress they cause; retail stays coalesced."
844	
845	**❓ What if the exchange feed itself goes down?**
846	The mechanism: fail over to a backup feed line; meanwhile serve last-value from the cache and flag prices as delayed on the UI. Our internal matching of our own book still functions; only external price freshness degrades.
847	🗣️ In plain words: if the price source dies we show the last known price marked 'delayed' and switch to a backup line.
848	💬 One-liner: "Backup feed line plus last-value cache with a 'delayed' flag — we never show a wrong price as live."
849	
850	> 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary: "It's a retail stock-trading platform where the sacred thing is correctness — orders placed in a fair order, money never lost or double-charged, portfolios that tell the truth. The design splits into two halves. The read half is a massive market-data edge: the exchange feed and our own fills are coalesced into per-symbol topics and fanned out over a fleet of WebSocket gateways to a million clients at sixty million messages a second, eventually consistent and fast. The write half is a small, strongly-consistent transaction core: orders are deduped, funds reserved in Postgres, stamped with a global sequence number, and matched by a single in-memory writer per symbol — that single-writer is the whole correctness story for price-time priority — with every event hitting a durable log so a crashed book replays in under a minute, and fills driving an exactly-once double-entry ledger. The two halves connect through that append-only event log, which feeds both the money ledger and the market-data feed from one ordered source. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: protect the correctness core at all costs, and let everything else be eventually consistent."

1	"""Capacity math for Design a Stock Trading Platform (Zerodha-style).

6	def r(n):
7	    """Round to a talkable order-of-magnitude string."""
8	    if n >= 1_000_000_000:
9	        return f"{n/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
10	    if n >= 1_000_000:
11	        return f"{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
12	    if n >= 1_000:
13	        return f"{n/1_000:.1f}K"
14	    return f"{n:.0f}"

16	print("=" * 64)

18	print("=" * 64)

20	# ---- Assumptions (locked in scope) ----
21	TOTAL_USERS = 20_000_000          # 20M registered (India retail broker scale)
22	DAU = 5_000_000                   # 5M daily active during market hours
23	ACTIVE_TRADERS = 3_000_000        # 3M who actually place orders on a given day
24	SYMBOLS = 5_000                   # tradable instruments (equity + F&O)
25	MARKET_HOURS_SEC = 6.25 * 3600    # 9:15-15:30 IST ~ 6h15m
26	SECONDS_DAY = 86_400
27	
28	print("\n-- INPUTS --")
29	print(f"Registered users         : {r(TOTAL_USERS)}")
30	print(f"Daily active users (DAU) : {r(DAU)}")
31	print(f"Active traders/day       : {r(ACTIVE_TRADERS)}")
32	print(f"Tradable symbols         : {r(SYMBOLS)}")
33	print(f"Market window (s)        : {MARKET_HOURS_SEC:.0f}")
34	
35	# ---- 1. Order WRITE QPS (place + cancel + modify) ----
36	ORDERS_PER_TRADER = 20            # avg orders/modifies/cancels per active trader per day
37	total_order_events = ACTIVE_TRADERS * ORDERS_PER_TRADER          # order events/day
38	avg_order_qps = total_order_events / MARKET_HOURS_SEC
39	# Bursts at open/close, on news, and on F&O expiry; brokers see ~10x average at
40	# the 9:15 open spike and far higher on a single hot contract (see #2).
41	BURST = 10
42	peak_order_qps = avg_order_qps * BURST
43	print("\n-- 1. ORDER WRITE QPS --")
44	print(f"Total order events/day   : {r(total_order_events)}")
45	print(f"Avg order write QPS      : {avg_order_qps:.0f}  (~{r(avg_order_qps)}/s)")
46	print(f"Peak order write QPS x{BURST}: {peak_order_qps:.0f}  (~{r(peak_order_qps)}/s)")
47	
48	# ---- 2. Per-symbol matching-engine throughput (the binding bottleneck) ----
49	# Orders are NOT uniform across symbols. On F&O expiry day a SINGLE index-option
50	# contract (e.g. the at-the-money weekly NIFTY option) takes a massive share of
51	# all order flow. This single hottest symbol is the binding bottleneck because
52	# its book MUST be processed by ONE writer to preserve price-time priority.
53	# Model the worst single symbol as taking ~30% of peak flow on expiry.
54	hot1_share = 0.30
55	hottest_symbol_peak_qps = peak_order_qps * hot1_share
56	SINGLE_WRITER_CEILING = 50_000    # one in-memory single-thread matcher ~ 50K orders/s/core
57	print("\n-- 2. PER-SYMBOL MATCHING ENGINE (binding bottleneck) --")
58	print(f"Hottest single symbol pk : {hottest_symbol_peak_qps:.0f}  (~{r(hottest_symbol_peak_qps)}/s)")
59	print(f"Single-writer shard ceil : {r(SINGLE_WRITER_CEILING)}/s/core")
60	print(f"Headroom on hottest sym  : {SINGLE_WRITER_CEILING/hottest_symbol_peak_qps:.1f}x")
61	# Flip threshold: when does ONE symbol exceed a single core?
62	flip_multiple = SINGLE_WRITER_CEILING / hottest_symbol_peak_qps
63	print(f"Flip: one symbol breaches single core at ~{r(SINGLE_WRITER_CEILING)}/s")
64	print(f"      i.e. if hottest-symbol flow grows ~{flip_multiple:.1f}x -> must split book")
65	
66	# ---- 3. Market-data fan-out (the read firehose) ----
67	# Every trade/quote update on every symbol fans out to subscribers. We do NOT
68	# push every raw tick: the edge COALESCES to a max delivery cadence per symbol
69	# (last-value-wins), so a client sees at most ~4 updates/sec/symbol however fast
70	# the book churns. This caps egress without losing the latest price.
71	RAW_TICK_PER_SYMBOL_SEC = 50          # raw book updates/sec on a hot symbol
72	total_tick_rate = SYMBOLS * RAW_TICK_PER_SYMBOL_SEC
73	CONCURRENT_STREAM_USERS = 1_000_000   # 1M users watching live quotes at peak
74	SYMBOLS_WATCHED_PER_USER = 15         # avg watchlist + open positions
75	COALESCED_RATE = 4                    # max delivered updates/sec/symbol after coalescing
76	msgs_per_user_sec = SYMBOLS_WATCHED_PER_USER * COALESCED_RATE
77	total_fanout_msgs_sec = CONCURRENT_STREAM_USERS * msgs_per_user_sec
78	print("\n-- 3. MARKET-DATA FAN-OUT (read firehose) --")
79	print(f"Raw exchange tick rate   : {r(total_tick_rate)}/s")
80	print(f"Concurrent stream users  : {r(CONCURRENT_STREAM_USERS)}")
81	print(f"Coalesced cadence        : {COALESCED_RATE}/s/symbol (last-value-wins)")
82	print(f"Msgs delivered/user/sec  : {msgs_per_user_sec}")
83	print(f"Total fan-out msgs/sec   : {r(total_fanout_msgs_sec)}/s")
84	WS_CONN_PER_NODE = 50_000

91	avg_portfolio_qps = portfolio_reads_day / MARKET_HOURS_SEC
92	peak_portfolio_qps = avg_portfolio_qps * BURST
93	CACHE_HIT = 0.90
94	miss_qps = peak_portfolio_qps * (1 - CACHE_HIT)
95	PG_REPLICA_QPS = 20_000           # read QPS a tuned PG read replica sustains
96	replicas = miss_qps / PG_REPLICA_QPS
97	print("\n-- 4. PORTFOLIO READ QPS (sized from MISS, not cache) --")
98	print(f"Portfolio reads/day      : {r(portfolio_reads_day)}")
99	print(f"Peak portfolio read QPS  : {peak_portfolio_qps:.0f}  (~{r(peak_portfolio_qps)}/s)")
100	print(f"Cache hit ratio          : {CACHE_HIT*100:.0f}%")
101	print(f"MISS QPS to DB           : {miss_qps:.0f}  (~{r(miss_qps)}/s)")
102	print(f"Read replicas (miss/repl): {replicas:.1f} -> round up")
103	print(f"Cold-cache (0% hit) worst: {peak_portfolio_qps:.0f}/s -> {peak_portfolio_qps/PG_REPLICA_QPS:.0f} replicas")

106	# Compare the read firehose (market-data deliveries) against order writes per sec.
107	rw_ratio = total_fanout_msgs_sec / peak_order_qps
108	print("\n-- 5. READ:WRITE RATIO (market-data deliveries : order writes) --")
109	print(f"Read:write ratio         : ~{r(rw_ratio)}:1")

113	ORDER_ROW_BYTES = 400             # order_id, user, symbol, side, qty, price, ts, state, ...
114	orders_per_year = total_order_events * 250    # ~250 trading days
115	order_idx_factor = 2.0            # secondary indexes (by user, by symbol, by state)
116	repl_factor = 3

121	print(f"Orders table/year (full) : {order_bytes_year/1e12:.1f} TB")

I looked at Dynamo — perfect for the idempotency cache — but it can't do the atomic double-entry I need."

Found a real numeric inconsistency. Line 682: "60M msgs/s ÷ 50K conns/node = ~20 nodes; **each node ~1.2M msgs/s**". The 20-node figure is correct (60M/3M per node), but "each node ~1.2M msgs/s" is wrong — 50K conns × 60 msgs/user/s = 3M msgs/node, and 60M/20 = 3M/node. The 1.2M would only be right with 50 nodes. This is a self-contradiction within the same sentence (20 nodes implies 3M/node, not 1.2M). MINOR/MAJOR — it's a stated capacity number that's internally contradictory.

Now let me examine the price-band splitting claim and a couple other technical correctness points more closely.

content.content:
594	#### Great: Sharded books + price-band splitting + replay recovery
595	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** one core per symbol caps a hot symbol and a crash loses the live book; this adds a way to split a single book and to recover fast.
596	**🗣️ Plain words:** keep one writer per book normally, but if a single contract gets insane, split its book into price bands each with its own writer, and recover a crashed book by replaying its log from the last snapshot.
597	**Approach:** (1) Books are assigned to shards by consistent hashing on symbol so we can rebalance without renumbering everything. (2) A hot symbol is detected by its per-shard QPS; we split its book into non-overlapping **price bands** (e.g. ≥2940 and <2940), each owned by one writer, with a thin coordinator that crosses only at the band boundary — preserving price-time within each band. (3) Recovery: a standby shard replays the event log from the last snapshot to rebuild the book in seconds, then resumes from the sequencer offset.
598	**Why this works:** keeps the single-writer invariant within a band while removing the single-core ceiling for the one symbol that needs it; replay bounds RTO.
599	
600	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** hottest symbol ~8K/s vs single-core ~50K/s = 6.3x headroom. Split triggers if sustained per-symbol QPS > ~40K/s (80% of ceiling). Two price bands roughly double the ceiling to ~100K/s for that one symbol. Replay: at ~50K events/s replay and a 1-min snapshot interval, worst-case ~3M events ≈ ~60s rebuild — within RTO.
601	
602	**✅ Failure matrix:**
603	
604	| Scenario | What happens |
605	|---|---|
606	| Order accepted, shard crashes before match | Replay from log rebuilds book; order is in the log, re-applied |
607	| Match done, log append fails | Fill NOT acknowledged; fail closed, no money moves |
608	| Shard crashes mid-snapshot | Snapshot is atomic-rename; partial snapshot ignored, replay from prior |
609	| Hot symbol > ceiling | Price-band split; if still over, throttle new orders (429) before dropping |
610	| Duplicate sequenced event | Single-writer enforces monotonic seq; duplicate ignored |
611	
612	> 🎙️ Script: "The heart is a single writer per symbol — one in-memory thread applying sequenced orders, no locks, so price-time priority is deterministic and a hot symbol at 8 thousand a second sits comfortably under a 50-thousand-a-second core. For crash safety, every event hits a durable log first and I snapshot books, so a standby rebuilds in under a minute by replay. The one escape hatch is if a single contract goes truly wild — I split its book into price bands, each with its own writer, which keeps ordering correct within a band while doubling the ceiling."
613	
614	> 🧠 If they ask "doesn't price-band splitting break price-time priority?": "Within a band it's fully preserved; orders only cross bands at the boundary, where a thin coordinator serializes the crossing. It's correct as long as bands don't overlap."
615

content.text:
The price-band splitting argument has a real technical subtlety the candidate partially addresses but the "doubles the ceiling" claim is questionable: matching inherently happens AT the spread (best bid crossing best ask), which is the boundary between the two bands.
